data.title: Delphi Digital: Five Future Trends in the Crypto Market
data.description:
Author: Delphi Digital; Translator: @Jinse Finance xz

Strategies that worked in previous cycles are no longer applicable. Some sectors of the market are performing exceptionally well, while the overall performance is stagnant. This has led to a significant divergence in investment returns, far removed from the shared profit experience that people are used to expecting.

This reflects both the market's maturation and a deeper structural shift.

1 No longer exclusively favored by capital

For many years, crypto assets have been the default destination for speculative capital seeking venture capital. This situation has now changed.

In 2024, private investment in generative artificial intelligence reached $35 billion, a 7.5-fold increase from 2022. In the first quarter of 2025 alone, robotics startups raised $2 billion. Today, every dollar of new liquidity faces competition from more growth assets available for investment. Crypto assets not only need to compete with other crypto projects, but also with all the cutting-edge technology narratives vying for speculative capital. The data clearly confirms this trend: Over the past 24 months, crypto stocks have outperformed most altcoins. Institutional capital's interest in spot ETFs and stocks like Coinbase, Robinhood, and Galaxy far exceeds its focus on the tokens themselves. Crypto stocks have effectively diverted some of the funding demand that might have flowed to altcoins. 2. The infrastructure layer is becoming commoditized. Meanwhile, the "fat protocol" theory is failing. This theory posits that the infrastructure layer will capture value, while the application layer will remain thin and fungible. This made sense in an era of scarce block space and protocols having pricing power. However, this theory no longer applies when data availability costs approach zero, the execution layer is fragmented by rollups and application chains, and the MEV (Maximum Extractable Value) is stripped away by application layer abstractions attempting to monopolize value. Real economic value is becoming the core metric—that is, the fees actually paid by users and flowing back to token holders (without driving growth through value dilution through issuance). 3. Macroeconomic Cycles and the Crypto Market The liquidity environment that has constrained the development of the crypto market over the past two years is reversing. However, whether the crypto market will respond remains to be seen. After withdrawing $2.4 trillion from the system since 2022, the Federal Reserve stopped quantitative tightening last December. Combined with market pricing already reflecting expectations of interest rate cuts continuing until 2026, this is the first net liquidity positive environment since the beginning of 2022. The Bitcoin/Gold ratio is currently at an all-time low. Gold has broken through +2 standard deviations in liquidity metrics, while Bitcoin's similar metrics are near bear market levels. The monthly relative strength index (RSI) for Bitcoin/Gold is in its most severe oversold condition ever. This weakness can be explained by several factors, including the threat of quantum computing, early whale selling, and the lack of market structure legislation. However, the data reveals a more fundamental mechanism: Bitcoin is currently negatively correlated with gold and Japanese 10-year government bonds, while gold and Japanese government bonds are positively correlated. Both gold and Japanese government bonds have experienced statistically significant fluctuations, deviating from historical norms by more than 2-3 standard deviations. When the market experiences such extreme volatility, the correlation pattern inevitably indicates that the related assets will exhibit abnormally weak performance. Bitcoin consistently lags behind gold. Once the situation regarding Japanese government bonds eases, and the Bank of Japan and the Federal Reserve find a way to stabilize carry trades, the pressure on gold and commodities will be alleviated—this may be Bitcoin's true window of opportunity. Stablecoins have become a core application scenario. Stablecoins have developed into the largest application scenario in the crypto space. Adjusted monthly stablecoin trading volume has surpassed PayPal and Visa. The total supply of stablecoins has exceeded $304 billion, a 33% increase compared to last year. Its reserves hold approximately $133 billion in U.S. Treasury bonds, making stablecoins the 19th largest holder of U.S. Treasury securities. The entire payment system is therefore being compressed. We are moving from the old world of card organizations, acquiring banks, issuing banks, and settlement cycles to a new era of programmable on-chain settlement. Stablecoins eliminate intermediary steps, simplify reconciliation processes, and internalize compliance as a native function rather than an afterthought. 5. Value Migrating Towards the Application Layer As infrastructure becomes commoditized and liquidity returns, value may flow to the application layer, which controls user relationships. Coinbase, Robinhood, Binance, and Hyperliquid are all heading towards the same destination from different starting points: the aggregation layer. The crypto space has a structural advantage in this regard. When Block (formerly Square) wanted to launch its "buy now, pay later" business, it had to spend $29 billion to acquire Afterpay. A crypto super app, however, only needs to integrate Aave to provide lending services and route perpetual contract transactions through Hyperliquid, earning a share through the builder code—without incurring acquisition costs. Leading fintech companies are taking a further step. Instead of leasing block space from existing public chains, they are building their own dedicated chains. Stripe launched the Tempo chain earlier this year, optimized for payment speed and using native stablecoins to pay gas fees; Robinhood is also following a similar path by building its own L2 chain for equity tokenization. In the past, on-chain activity lacked diverse scenarios beyond directional token speculation. Now, ecosystems such as prediction markets (via Polymarket), on-chain lending, and deep perpetual contract liquidity have taken shape. All functions that meet an individual's financial needs can be accessed through a single interface. Real-world asset tokenization is a key driver of this process. In January 2025, the value of on-chain tokenized stocks was approximately $15 million; it has now surpassed $500 million. Anyone can hold equity assets through a self-custodied wallet without a brokerage account. The development of equity perpetual contracts is even more rapid. Platforms such as Hyperliquid, Ostium Labs, and VestExchange now offer leveraged exposure to stocks, indices, commodities, and forex. CFD brokers generate hundreds of billions of dollars in daily trading volume outside the US, and retail options flows increasingly reflect purely directional bets rather than volatility trading—perpetual contracts are a purer tool for meeting both of these needs. As a result, brokerage accounts are quietly being replaced by self-custodied wallets. Stocks, cryptocurrencies, forex, and commodities coexist on a single interface, with settlement, margining, and custody all operating on crypto infrastructure. The era of passively allocating "crypto assets" as an asset class is over. Bitcoin is emerging as a self-contained macro asset, decoupling from the crypto market. Protocols that truly capture fees are consistently outperforming the market; narratives alone are no longer sufficient. Stablecoin infrastructure is becoming the bridge connecting the crypto world to everything else.

data.title: YouTube star Dan Koe's long post: How to fix your life in one day
data.description:
Author: Annie Mei  Image: Dan Koe's Twitter image

Recently, I've been researching some independent thinkers, and Dan Koe is one of the representatives I greatly admire.

He has 1.26 million subscribers on YouTube and 790,000 followers on Twitter (X).

In just four years, he went from earning $3,000 a month to $4.2 million a year. His success is not an unattainable myth, but a path that every ordinary person can learn from.

He just sent a tweet today, which impressed me very much:

The best thing you can do in today's world is practice thinking.

Very few people, especially smart people, do this.

Snap yourself out of your automatic patterns and stretch your mind for once.

This will only become more important as AI and social media distort reality.

Translation is:

I. The reason you're not in the place you want to go is because you're not yet "the person who will be there."

Most people, when they think of change, their first reaction is:Change actions → Set goals → Force self-discipline.

The result is often: relying on motivation for a few weeks, and then returning to square one.

Dan said: Changing your goals isn't the most important thing; changing "who you are" is.

Dan said:Changing your goals isn't the most important thing; changing "who you are" is.

For example: Do bodybuilders need to "force" themselves to maintain a healthy diet? Do CEOs need to "force" themselves to take responsibility? They don't feel pain because that's their lifestyle and identity. Dan is often described as "too disciplined," and even his mother tells him, "You should relax." His answer is: If I don't find it interesting, why should I do what I'm doing? If you want to achieve a certain result, you must begin living a life that will produce that result before you even arrive at it. If you want health, start living a healthy life now; if you want a certain kind of life, start living that identity now. Secondly, the reason you're not going to the places you want to go is because you don't actually want to go there. This statement sounds counterintuitive at first glance. "I clearly want to be rich, how could it be that I don't?" Dan explained this from the perspective of "brain operation mechanisms." In *The Miracle Power of Mind-Body Unity*, Galway proposed a concept: the self has two levels. Self 1: Consciousness, mind, reason; Self 2: Body, subconscious, instinct. Often, it is Self 1 that wants to go there, while Self 2 resists. For example, procrastination. The apparent reason is "I lack self-discipline," but the real goal may be: to avoid the risks of being judged, criticized, or noticed.

Third, the reason you're not going to the places you want to go is because you're afraid of actually getting there

Dan gives a very important "identity formation cycle":

  1. You want to achieve a goal

  2. You see the world through the "lens" of this goal

  3. You only pay attention to information (learning) that is useful to the goal

  4. Act and get feedback

data.title: On-chain is not a tax haven: CARF closes in, launching a global tax crackdown on crypto assets.
data.description:
Author: danny; Source: X, @agintender

Who would have thought that the modern multinational tax information system was caused by a tube of toothpaste? A UBS banker smuggled diamonds across the border by stuffing them into a toothpaste tube—a scene reminiscent of Hollywood—unexpectedly tolled the death knell for Swiss banking secrecy laws. Now, the gears of history are relentlessly grinding towards the crypto world—that once-hidden "tax haven" is about to face its reckoning.

This article will unveil the mystery of CARF: it's the closing net of a global tax operation. From Binance's strategic move to relocate to the UAE to buy time, to the harsh reality that "crypto-to-crypto trading" is no longer tax-free; from Hong Kong's compliance countdown to the shattering of mainland investors' wishful thinking.

... This is not only a reshaping of the industry landscape, but also a survival guide that every crypto asset holder must face—after all, in this algorithm-woven cage, no one can continue to bury their head in the sand like an ostrich. Introduction: What is CARF? CARF stands for Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework. Its core mechanism is that Crypto Asset Service Providers (RCASPs) with reporting obligations collect tax-related information about their clients and related transactions, and submit it to the tax authorities in their respective jurisdictions. Ultimately, this information is automatically exchanged internationally between tax authorities. This is similar to CRS in the traditional financial sector, but CARF specifically focuses on the buying, selling, exchanging, custody, and transfer of crypto assets. Simply put, previously, when users traded cryptocurrencies on exchanges, the tax authorities of their country of residence had difficulty fully grasping the relevant information. CARF now connects users' tax residency countries with the jurisdictions of exchanges. Once a CARF partnership is established, the user's tax residency country can obtain detailed information about its tax residents' overseas cryptocurrency trading activities and conduct tax collection accordingly. As of the end of 2025, more than 75 jurisdictions have committed to implementing CARF in 2027 or 2028, with over half having signed relevant competent authority agreements. From January 1, 2026, the CARF framework will be implemented in the first batch of 48 jurisdictions, covering the UK, EU, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, and other regions.

Chapter 1, Diamonds in Toothpaste, The End of Secrecy and the Arrival of CRS

To understand CARF's "new scythe," we must first look at the "old fishing net"—CRS (Common Reporting Standard).

The protagonist of the story is Bradley Birkenfeld, formerly a major account manager at UBS.

To smuggle $200 million in untaxed assets belonging to his client, American real estate tycoon Igor Olenicoff, from UBS back to the United States without leaving a trace, Birkenfeld devised a plot only a Hollywood screenwriter would dare to use: he purchased diamonds, stuffed them into a tube of ordinary toothpaste to evade customs X-rays, and then brazenly flew across the Atlantic to deliver the diamonds to Olenicoff for liquidation. In 2007, when Birkenfeld discovered he might become a scapegoat for an internal compliance purge in a bank report, he made a decision that defied the traditions of Swiss banking: he defected. He walked into the door of the U.S. Department of Justice with a list of top-secret internal emails and client names. Birkenfeld's testimony directly led to UBS paying a record-breaking $780 million fine in 2009 and unprecedentedly handing over a list of more than 4,000 American clients. This marked the death of Swiss bank secrecy laws. (Interestingly, Birkenfeld ultimately took home a bounty of 104 million.) The US Congress realized that relying on informants like Birkenfeld was far from sufficient; an automated monitoring mechanism was necessary. Thus, in 2010, the most authoritative tax law in history, the Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA), was enacted. Its logic was simple and brutal: "Banks worldwide that want to do business with the United States must report the account balances of Americans to us annually." Seeing the immediate effectiveness of this US approach, the OECD began to replicate it. In 2014, the global standard based on FATCA—the Common Reporting Standard (CRS)—was officially born. This is why the underlying logic of CRS is very similar to checking bank statements: it assumes that wealth will eventually accumulate in bank accounts, generating interest and forming a balance. It's a monitoring system tailor-made for the "fiat currency era," aiming to expose hidden millionaires through an annual "balance snapshot." Just as everything was moving in the direction regulators hoped for, a new phenomenon called Bitcoin was quietly emerging. This CRS system, based on "balance monitoring," was about to face a completely new adversary it had never imagined. Chapter Two: The Vulnerabilities of the Old Hunting Net—Why CARF When CRS Exists? Using an AI analogy, CARF is like a high-definition camera installed 24/7 at the entrance of every compliant exchange. Its biggest difference from CRS is that CRS checks "how much money you have," while CARF checks "where you've spent your money." 2.1 The Origin and Strategic Intent of CARF The birth of CARF stemmed from the G20 countries' fear of tax base erosion. While the traditional CRS has been effective in combating offshore tax evasion, it primarily targets traditional bank accounts and custodian accounts. Crypto assets, due to their decentralized nature and peer-to-peer transfer capabilities without intermediaries, have become a blind spot for CRS. The OECD has explicitly stated that CARF aims to eliminate this blind spot by including crypto asset service providers (CASPs) in the same information reporting obligations as banks. By the end of 2025, more than 50 jurisdictions (including the UK, Canada, France, Germany, Japan, and the Cayman Islands) have committed to implementing CARF. This framework quietly began data collection in the Cayman Islands and other locations on January 1, 2026, and will conduct its first information exchange in 2027. 2.2 Comparison of CARF and CRS 2.0: From "Stock" to "Circulation" The core logic of CRS is to monitor "stock wealth," while the core logic of CARF is to monitor the circulation of wealth. Under the CRS framework, apart from the year-end balance, the tax authorities can hardly see the intermediate processes. However, under CARF, if an investor converts Bitcoin into USDT, transfers USDT to their cold wallet, or even uses cryptocurrency to purchase more than $50,000 worth of $PUNDIAI (retail payment transactions), each action will generate a report record. CARF effectively elevates the perspective from a "static balance sheet" to a "dynamic cash flow statement."

2.3 Scope of “Related Crypto Assets”

CARF’s definition of “related crypto assets” covers almost all crypto assets:

Stablecoins: Although many stablecoins claim to be alternatives to fiat currency, under CARF, they are explicitly considered crypto assets. This means that an exchange between USDT and USD may no longer be considered a “currency exchange” but a transaction, and such a transaction is a taxable event.

NFTs: While CARF primarily focuses on assets used for payments or investments, most high-value NFTs are likely to be included in the reporting scope due to their secondary market trading attributes. Tokenized Securities: Even tokenized stocks or bonds already regulated in traditional financial markets, even if they are on-chain, may be subject to both CRS and CARF coverage (although the OECD has attempted to avoid duplicate reporting by revising CRS, this overlap is difficult to avoid due to the principle of "better to err on the side of caution" in tax practice). Chapter 3. Retail Investors' Sentimentality, Wishful Thinking, and Devastation 3.1 Crypto-to-Crypto Trading: A Mandatory "Fair Price" Mechanism CARF stipulates that all exchanges between crypto assets must record their fair market value in fiat currency at the moment the transaction occurs. In the eyes of the tax authorities, "crypto-to-crypto trading" is equivalent to "selling before buying." A common misconception is: "If I exchange Bitcoin for Ethereum, as long as I don't convert it to fiat currency (USD/CNY), it doesn't count as a sale, and I don't need to pay tax." But this is just wishful thinking on the part of novice investors. CARF requires exchanges to record: "On a certain day, Zhang San exchanged 1 Bitcoin for 20 Ethereum. At that time, 1 Bitcoin was worth $50,000." In the eyes of the tax authorities, this is a taxable event of "selling Bitcoin for $50,000." Even though you don't have cash on hand, your tax bill has already been generated. CARF has completely ended the tax avoidance strategy of "using cryptocurrency to generate more cryptocurrency." After 2026 (2027 in some regions), every cryptocurrency exchange will be recorded as an asset disposal event, leaving a definite "fiat currency gain record" in your tax file, regardless of whether you convert it into fiat currency/stablecoin. 3.2 Penetrating Wallets: Transaction Hash and Address Cleaning In CARF's XML Schema, RCASP is required to report the specific type and value of transactions. Although the final rule, after strong lobbying from the industry, removed the mandatory requirement to report all non-custodial wallet receiving addresses, the internal system collects and retains information about this address and its associated beneficiaries for at least 5 years (aka the "retention rule"). This means that the tax authorities have the right to access the data at any time. If the tax authorities find that a taxpayer has a large "withdrawal" record in 2026 but has not declared subsequent income, they can send bulk information requests to exchanges to accurately obtain these external wallet addresses. When you withdraw coins from an exchange to your own wallet plugin or cold wallet, the exchange must record and report (if requested) "which address was withdrawn". This is similar to withdrawing cash from a bank. The bank not only records how much you withdrew but also tracks you and notes which safe you put the money in. Once your wallet address and your real identity are linked in the tax bureau's database, all your DeFi operations on the blockchain are essentially "naked." 3.3 Standardization of Valuation Anchoring What if the transaction involves two extremely obscure cryptocurrencies (e.g., exchanging "air coin A" for "air coin B"), and there's no fiat currency trading pair? CARF stipulates a "cascading valuation method": if asset A has no fiat currency price, the fiat currency price of asset B is referenced; if neither exists, the service provider must use a reasonable valuation method to enforce pricing based on this. In short, the system must generate a fiat currency value and send it to the tax bureau. This eliminates the possibility for users to use price fluctuations to make vague declarations when filing taxes. 3.4 Mandatory Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) CARF requires RCASP to collect users' tax residency and corresponding Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN). However, if a user only declares their tax residency in a lower-tax jurisdiction (such as Dubai), but the exchange discovers through IP address, area code, or login logs that they frequently operate in a higher-tax jurisdiction (such as France), the exchange has an obligation to question the legitimacy of this self-certification. Chapter 4, The Trap of Retrospection: 2026 as the "Year of Exposure" Many veteran investors believe that as long as they handle their assets before the first information exchange in 2027, everything will be fine. This is incorrect. Everyone overlooks CARF's "retrospective effect," meaning that the 2027 information exchange implies submitting information from 2026. 4.1 “Beginning Balance” and Historical Audit When the Inland Revenue Department (IRD) receives the CARF data for the entire year of 2026 in 2027, they will first focus on the “Beginning Balance” or “Total Annual Transactions.” Scenario Simulation: Assume that Mr. Nakamoto, a Chinese investor, sold $10 million worth of $PUNDIAI tokens through a compliant Hong Kong platform in 2026. The platform reports the data to the IRD based on CARF data. The IRD's AI system will immediately compare Mr. Nakamoto's personal income tax returns from 2025 onwards. If Mr. Nakamoto has never previously declared holding overseas crypto assets, the source of this $10 million becomes a huge question. The IRD will use the hash value of this transaction to trace back to when these $PUNDIAI tokens were purchased. If the purchase was made in 2024, then all unreported capital gains between 2024 and 2026 will be exposed. It's worth noting that tax authorities in many countries have deployed AI-based big data analytics systems specifically designed to identify anomalies where asset holdings don't match reported income. We anticipate a major tax evasion crisis for crypto millionaires in 2026. 4.2 The Compliance Window in 2026 For investors who haven't yet complied, 2026 is effectively the last window of opportunity. Before the data gates close, investors face a difficult choice: Proactively reporting historical assets to the tax authorities can usually lead to a reduction or exemption of penalties. Under a compliant framework (such as family trusts or offshore companies), reorganize asset holdings or seek assistance from professional financial and tax institutions to rationally plan crypto assets. (Advertisement here, ad space is hotly tendered~) Chapter 5, Behind Binance's Relocation: Trading Space for Time Among a host of regulatory-friendly jurisdictions, why did Binance ultimately choose Abu Dhabi? Besides local policy support and advantages in funding channels, another important factor is the **compliance time difference**. Binance's original location in the Cayman Islands is among the first jurisdictions to commit to implementing CARF, with the first information exchange expected in 2027. This means that Crypto Service Providers (RCASPs) with CARF reporting obligations will need to collect and retain information for reporting starting in 2026. If Binance remains based in the Cayman Islands, it must immediately begin building a comprehensive CARF compliance system. Conversely, the UAE, according to the CARF implementation timeline, is among the second batch of jurisdictions to implement CARF and plans to begin information exchange in 2028. From the Cayman Islands to the UAE, Binance has secured a one-year strategic buffer period. For Binance, which serves over 300 million users, this period is of great significance: First, it mitigates early adopter risks. By observing how the first implementing jurisdictions like the UK and the Cayman Islands operate, Binance can learn from the experiences of other exchanges and optimize its own compliance strategy. Second, it allows participation in rule-making. Currently, the UAE's CARF legislation and implementation details are still being formulated. As a leading exchange with considerable influence, Binance has the opportunity to voice its opinions, consult with authorities, and exert a positive influence on the formation of localized rules. Third, it allows for system upgrades. This year provides Binance with ample time to deploy and debug a data reporting and management system that meets the complex requirements of CARF. This is what is known as "trading space for time." Chapter 6. CARF in China: Impact and Trends As one of the world's largest markets for crypto asset users, China's situation is somewhat unique. Some say that mainland China is not among the first signatories of the OECD's CARF, so mainland tax authorities cannot see cryptocurrency transactions conducted in Hong Kong—this is actually a misunderstanding. Mainland China has not yet joined or committed to implementing CARF, therefore mainland tax authorities will not obtain crypto asset transaction data of Chinese tax residents based on the CARF mechanism. However, this does not mean that mainland crypto tycoons can rest easy. Besides, mainland China is already an active participant in CRS. Although CARF targets crypto assets, if crypto assets are converted into fiat currency and deposited in banks, or held in the form of financial assets (such as ETFs), they are already within the CRS monitoring network. Furthermore, the consultation document also mentions that CARF information will be exchanged with "partner jurisdictions." Attentive readers will notice that Hong Kong is in the second tier of implementing CARF, having already initiated legislative consultations on CARF and CRS revisions and formulated a clear implementation roadmap, planning to complete legislative preparations in 2027 and begin information exchange in 2028. Under the "dual-track" crypto regulatory framework, the impact of CARF's implementation in China needs to be considered in different ways: Crypto users with Hong Kong identities are obligated to submit self-certified information to exchanges under the CARF framework. Subsequently, their crypto asset transaction data on overseas exchanges will be reported and exchanged with the Hong Kong tax authorities through an automatic exchange mechanism. This means increased transparency in assets and transactions, making it more difficult for users to evade tax obligations by leveraging the decentralized and anonymous nature of crypto transactions. Simultaneously, Hong Kong crypto exchanges, as RCASPs (Regulatory Service Providers), must strengthen KYC (Know Your Customer) requirements and establish data collection and reporting systems in accordance with CARF requirements. Failure to register, declare, conduct due diligence, or submit inaccurate information at any stage can trigger legal liability, with fines reaching millions of Hong Kong dollars. In contrast, the impact of CARF on mainland China in the short term is relatively limited. This is related to the mainland's "illegal" characterization of crypto assets. However, transparency in crypto taxation is an inevitable trend, and mainland Chinese tax residents cannot be complacent. With Hong Kong's access to CARF's global information exchange network, it is possible that mainland China will obtain relevant crypto transaction data from Hong Kong through other channels, or join CARF in the future. For mainland Chinese investors, the era of relying on Hong Kong as a "safe haven" is over. Although there may be a time lag of several years for automatic exchange, the "on-demand exchange" channel is open, and data retention rules ensure that historical records are readily accessible. Chapter 7, Survival Guide – Don't Be an Ostrich Burying Its Head in the Sand. If you ask a Korean oppa what the three unavoidable things in this world are: life and death, Samsung (a Korean entertainment brand), and taxes. As individuals caught in this tide of the times, what should we do? **Pay Attention to the Tax Consequences of Cryptocurrency Trading:** Don't naively think that you don't have to pay taxes if you don't withdraw funds. From now on, every click of "buy/sell" carries the potential for taxation. (In countries with capital gains tax) **Clean Up Your Accounts:** Clean up those "zombie accounts" on unknown small exchanges or registered with random identities. Either cancel them or withdraw your coins. Once the CARF network is fully operational, these accounts will be among the first to be subject to risk control. Understanding Cold Wallets: Cold wallets remain your last line of defense for your data, but the bridge between them is now under surveillance. When you transfer funds from Binance to a cold wallet, the transaction itself becomes a record. While the tax authorities cannot see everything in a cold wallet, they know: "This address belongs to Nakamoto Murao, who transferred 10 bitcoins into it in 2027." Pay Attention to the UAE and Hong Kong Timelines: The UAE and Hong Kong are among the second batch of regions to implement the policy (swap in 2028). This means you have roughly a year or two to adapt and plan. Using this time to learn how to comply with regulations or to find a professional tax advisor is far more practical than searching for the next "tax haven."

......

Postscript

This article thanks FinTax for their professional analysis of tax regulations and their observations and analyses of various jurisdictions, which enriched the practical perspective of this article.

data.title: RWA Outlook 2026: Macroeconomics, Liquidity, and Market Reach
data.description:
Author: CoinFound & DigiFT

Overview

2025 was a breakthrough year for the tokenization of Real World Assets (RWAs), marking the space's transition from pilot phases to early institutional scale. Issuers significantly expanded the types of tokenized products, including money market funds, private lending, commodities, and emerging equity tokenization models; meanwhile, the total value locked in on-chain RWAs continued to rise.

Entering 2026, market opportunities are no longer just a "continuation of 2025".

... The core of the next phase lies in accelerating development through market structure: a clearer regulatory framework, broader institutional participation, continuously increasing investor demand, and expansion into more asset classes. A key change is that Web3 participants are no longer limited to crypto native users. Users' asset allocation thinking is becoming more diversified: they want decentralized exposure, simpler access paths, and faster settlement speeds—often not viewing "blockchain" itself as an investment target. Looking ahead, Web3 is gradually evolving from a single asset class concept into a distribution technology. Macroeconomic Environment Drives the Market To understand the factors that may drive RWA development in 2026, we first need to review the driving factors behind the changes in risk appetite in 2025. Digital assets (including crypto assets and RWA) do not exist in isolation; they are increasingly affected by the same macroeconomic variables as traditional markets—including interest rates, inflation trends, policy uncertainty, and the global liquidity environment. US policy and macroeconomic control will remain key determining factors in 2026, as they anchor global liquidity and set the tone for risk-free rates. The Federal Reserve's (Fed) long-term inflation target is 2%, typically measured by the Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index (PCE), which has a broader scope and better reflects actual household spending than the Consumer Price Index (CPI). Nevertheless, the CPI remains the most widely followed inflation indicator by the market and the public, serving as a "headline" reference for price pressures. As of December 2025, US CPI inflation is projected at 2.7% year-on-year, with an unemployment rate of approximately 4.4%. This combination fuels ongoing market discussions regarding the timing and pace of further interest rate cuts. In December 2025, the Fed will implement its third interest rate cut of the year—the sixth since September 2024. Reuters also points out that the Federal Reserve has cumulatively cut interest rates by 75 basis points in 2025, and at the beginning of 2026, policymakers have indicated they prefer to wait for more data before taking further action. What does this mean for investors? Lower interest rates will have a substantial impact on investor behavior in both traditional and on-chain markets. If monetary policy easing accelerates, especially in a more aggressive scenario (such as when Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran considers a rate cut of "more than 100 basis points" reasonable), front-end yields are likely to compress significantly. In this scenario, the yield on short-term U.S. Treasury bonds could fall to around 2.5%–3.0% by the end of the year (depending on the speed and magnitude of rate cuts), a level of yield that will be significantly less attractive to many investors compared to broader investment opportunities. When cash-like yields decline, some investors typically rebalance their portfolios, reducing their concentrated allocation to Treasury bonds and US dollar money market funds, and instead opting for assets with higher expected returns or diversified returns. In practice, this often manifests as a increased preference for gold (as an inflation hedge), as well as selective asset allocation to credit assets (including private credit) and real estate, depending on risk appetite and the macroeconomic environment. The same dynamic adjustment mechanism applies to digital assets. When risk-free rates decline and liquidity conditions improve, Bitcoin (BTC) may benefit from renewed capital inflows, including through compliant channels such as Bitcoin spot ETFs. In an environment of increased macroeconomic uncertainty, the narrative of "Bitcoin as digital gold/hedge" often re-emerges (even if not always in the short term). Historical experience shows that when Bitcoin strengthens due to improved risk sentiment or capital inflows, high-beta tokens typically follow suit, although performance varies significantly among different altcoins. Importantly, on-chain investors are no longer limited to cryptocurrency allocations. With the tokenization of real-world assets, market participants are increasingly able to allocate traditional assets—including interest rates, credit, commodities, and stocks—on-chain. This convergence presents different types of investors with the same core asset allocation challenges: where will the next sustainable sources of return and diversification lie when money market fund and short-term Treasury yields fall to lower levels? Under current institutional and operational constraints, which assets offer greater accessibility and holdability? This raises a crucial question: which RWA asset classes are most likely to experience breakthrough growth in 2026? Asset Classes to Watch in 2026 The tokenization of money market funds and private lending will continue to grow in 2026, primarily because issuers and allocators have gradually understood how these products can be integrated into existing compliance structures, custody, and operational processes. Meanwhile, experience from 2025 shows that once a tokenized project is successfully implemented and validated in one or two relatively robust asset classes, issuers typically increase their willingness to expand to adjacent products within the same asset class. From a distribution perspective, the market is seeing increasing active demand, no longer limited to cash management or credit allocation, but extending to commodities, equities (including non-US markets), and differentiated strategies. This is crucial because it will determine which structures will be prioritized, which partners will be mobilized, and which asset classes will be able to scale first. The market structure itself is also maturing. Current tokenization platforms include both single-asset specialization and multi-asset distribution platforms, and this diversification is expanding the scope of assets that can be tokenized. For 2026, the core issue is no longer "whether assets can be tokenized," but rather how tokenization can truly expand asset accessibility within a framework of clear permissions, legal constraints, scalable operations, and effective liquidity/transfer mechanisms. The following asset classes deserve special attention because they simultaneously possess: (i) clear investor demand, (ii) growing demand for multi-asset allocation, and (iii) continuously improving distribution channels, making previously difficult-to-access assets more accessible. Precious Metals: More Than Just Gold Historically, tokenized commodities have been dominated by gold, primarily due to its mature custody and collateral system and its long-standing status as a globally recognized store of value. However, the next phase of growth may extend to a broader range of precious metals. In December 2025, silver, platinum, and palladium prices all hit record highs due to increased market uncertainty and investors seeking asset diversification. Just as investors are no longer limiting their precious metal allocations to gold, tokenized issuers are likely to more actively expand into these metals to meet investor diversification needs and their growing confidence. However, the current market structure remains unbalanced. Gold already has established leading products and distribution channels, such as Tether Gold (XAU₮) and PAX Gold (PAXG), both clearly anchored to physical gold custody and redemption frameworks. In contrast, other precious metal tokenized products have not yet established the same level of brand influence and distribution depth. If new projects can combine credible collateral standards with sustained liquidity, precious metal tokenization is expected to expand further in 2026. Furthermore, early indications suggest that on-chain participants are simultaneously trading precious metal-linked assets and equity-based RWAs. In 2025, the three most traded RWA stock/ETF tokens on the Bitget platform were pegged to Tesla (TSLAon), iShares Gold Trust (IAUon), and iShares Silver Trust (SLVon), respectively. Real estate represents one of the largest tokenization opportunities in the long term due to its massive size and structural issues such as high investor barriers and friction in transaction processes, which tokenization has the potential to alleviate. Deloitte predicts that the tokenized real estate market could reach $4 trillion by 2035, although this is still small compared to the total global real estate value—Savills estimates the total global real estate value at approximately $393 trillion as of early 2025. Looking ahead to 2026, if interest rates decline, mortgage affordability and the overall financing environment are expected to improve marginally, potentially supporting real estate transaction activity and asset prices in most markets. In this context, the appeal of tokenized real estate lies not in replacing property ownership, but in serving as a new distribution and access channel, particularly suitable for investors seeking smaller investments, cross-border participation, or faster transfer mechanisms. Tokenized US stocks are gradually emerging. Although the current market size is still small, institutional discussions increasingly point to tokenized stocks potentially becoming an important supplementary distribution and liquidity channel in the long term. A Bitget report indicates that 95% of tokenized stock traders are also cryptocurrency holders. A key development in 2025 was the rapid emergence of various equity tokenization models in the market. These models need to be clearly distinguished because they optimize for different purposes (e.g., access and liquidity vs. shareholder rights and corporate governance): First, there are price/economic exposure tokens (such as Backed's xStocks and Ondo Global Markets), used to track the economic performance of listed stocks rather than granting shareholder rights; and direct issuance structures (such as Superstate's direct issuance projects), giving investors more direct access to the issuance/registration process; and the compliant on-chain equity market structure driven by Securitize. Currently, equity tokenization remains primarily focused on publicly traded company equity, which is why private equity is considered the "next focus" for 2026. Many investors want earlier access to high-quality private companies, and tokenization can broadly reach them through early employee stock ownership, VC/PE funds, or other structured methods. For holders and funds, the core value lies in liquidity—built on the proven legal and operational foundation of public equity tokenization, tokenization is expected to gradually support the establishment of secondary transfers and on-chain liquidity pathways. Over the past two years, artificial intelligence (AI) and the technology industry have been significant drivers of market performance, and the landscape in 2026 remains favorable for continued innovation and mergers and acquisitions. The problem is that most value creation occurs in the pre-IPO stage, making it difficult for ordinary investors to participate. Meta's acquisition of Manus for over $2 billion clearly demonstrates how private market opportunities can be quickly repriced by strategic buyers, thus disappearing from public accessibility. This is why tokenized private equity is increasingly seen as the "next frontier" of RWA—its significance lies not only in earlier access but also in expanding the pool of eligible investors through compliant distribution channels. Hedge Fund Strategies Currently, RWA products in the market are mainly concentrated in cash management and credit assets, leaving investors willing to take on higher risks with limited high-yield options. Therefore, tokenized hedge fund strategies are worth watching in 2026. While these strategies may have lower liquidity than the "core" RWA category, they are more attractive to investors with a higher risk tolerance, with some strategies targeting returns of approximately 15%–30%. In 2025, an AIMA and PwC report indicated that about one-third of hedge funds were actively pursuing or planning to explore tokenization, with the strongest interest coming from Asia and the Middle East. The report also showed that 52% of surveyed hedge funds expressed varying degrees of interest in tokenized fund structures, primarily driven by the desire to expand investor access and improve operational efficiency. A common question is: if liquidity is limited, will investors still buy tokenized hedge funds? The key is not that tokenization automatically creates "liquidity," but rather that it can gradually support secondary market liquidity—through over-the-counter transfers facilitated by market makers, or through integration with DeFi once infrastructure and compliance mature. Furthermore, liquidity is not the only concern for investors; in a market environment where uncertainty persists, investors also seek differentiated, high-yield strategies that can benefit from volatility. Non-US Equities and Regional Inclusivity From a global perspective, the Japanese and Chinese stock markets have appeared relatively "dormant" for years, but their actual performance has been strong and is expected to expand further in 2026. China continues to innovate in the technology and AI sectors, while the Japanese stock market is regaining global investor attention, and the USD/JPY exchange rate also has a significant impact on cross-border allocation and return expectations. Even Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway has publicly stated its long-term holding of Japanese stocks. However, the biggest problem currently lies in accessibility. In China, foreign investment in A-shares has long been low, typically accounting for only about 3-4% of the total market capitalization, reflecting structural limitations such as capital controls, the QFII quota system, and limited access channels for foreign capital. Although access has improved in recent years, the proportion of foreign ownership remains limited, and the domestic stock market is still considered an inaccessible market by many global investors. The situation in Japan is different. Foreign investment in the Japanese stock market is quite high, although not necessarily "at its limit," and is generally considered to hold about 30% of the market capitalization, although there are significant differences across different sectors. Foreign capital inflows are expected to be particularly significant in 2025. Reuters reported that in one week in October 2025, foreign investors made net purchases of 752.6 billion yen (approximately $5 billion) in Japanese stocks, pushing the Nikkei index near a record high. The Financial Times also reported that in April 2025, foreign investors purchased 8.2 trillion yen in Japanese stocks and bonds in a single month, a new high since at least 2005. This indicates that the Japanese stock market is relatively open to entry, and demand is still growing. If entry paths are further simplified, participation could increase further. A potential solution is to tokenize Chinese and Japanese stocks. As the legal framework matures, in 2026, foreign investors may be able to access a wider range of non-US stocks through on-chain mechanisms, no longer limited to the US stock market. Key Factors Driving RWA Adoption in 2026 While 2025 may be the most important year for tokenization to date, the market is still in its early stages. If 2025 was a turning point from the experimental phase to institutional-scale momentum, then 2026 has the potential to be the year when tokenization truly deeply integrates with the on-chain ecosystem. The primary factor driving RWA adoption is: deep integration with the on-chain ecosystem. The main obstacle facing DeFi protocols and RWA issuers is legal and compliance alignment. Some DeFi protocols do not yet have the structural conditions to integrate RWA, and the issuance structure of some RWAs also limits their integration possibilities. DeFi protocols need viable solutions for holding security tokens, such as through custodians or by establishing entities that can hold RWA. From the issuer's perspective, if DeFi protocols cannot directly hold security tokens, issuers still need to build on-chain composability by constructing solutions. Feasible paths include building a permissionless access layer under compliance requirements, or partnering with a vault architecture possessing asset custody capabilities, whereby the latter holds the underlying security tokens and issues certificate-type tokens representing the rights and interests of the underlying assets. This is precisely why market structure legislation is crucial. The U.S. Clarity Act on Digital Asset Markets has sparked widespread discussion, with its core objective being to establish clearer regulatory boundaries for the U.S. crypto market and clarify the responsibilities of various regulatory agencies and market participants. If the market structure is clarified, trading platforms and protocols will more easily meet compliance expectations at the operational level, thus paving the way for the integration process of real-world asset tokenization. A noteworthy current situation is that most DeFi protocols are currently closer to technology service providers than financial intermediaries. This characteristic makes the importance of legal compliance alignment increasingly prominent in the field of real-world asset tokenization as integration deepens. In the issuance and distribution of security tokens, integration through regulated channels is a key consideration—especially in first-tier jurisdictions. This necessity stems not only from macroeconomic uncertainties but also directly from regulatory risks. In practice, trading platforms without the necessary licenses constantly face the risk of being restricted or shut down by regulatory agencies, leading to complex issues for investors such as limited liquidity and disorganized exit mechanisms. For example, the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) has issued guidelines clarifying the regulatory requirements for tokenized capital market products under the Securities and Futures Act. Depending on the token's attributes and the specific type of activity, related distribution activities may trigger licensing requirements. While regulation cannot completely eliminate risks, regulated pathways typically provide market participants with a more robust operating framework than unregulated venues. Finally, the issue of regulatory arbitrage needs attention. Traditionally, regulatory arbitrage refers to exploiting or circumventing regulatory differences between jurisdictions to circumvent restrictions or gain an unequal competitive advantage. In practice, market access is the primary constraint, and the most direct way to overcome these restrictions is usually to find a jurisdiction that allows such operations—for example, by opening a brokerage account in Hong Kong to access assets that are difficult to allocate directly in the domestic market. While tokenization can broaden asset access channels, such assets are still classified as securities in most jurisdictions, meaning that compliance boundaries still apply. The only way to completely overcome these restrictions is to convert assets into a permissionless form and incorporate them into the DeFi system—this brings us back to the core issue: the on-chain ecosystem integration of RWA will be the most critical factor driving RWA adoption in 2026. In summary, the key in 2026 is not whether RWA can be tokenized, but whether it can be distributed and used at scale—with clearly defined rights, an enforceable legal framework, and operational processes that support custody, settlement, and transfer. As cash yields normalize, investment demand may expand from money markets and credit tokens to commodities, stocks, real estate, and differentiated strategies, particularly in areas where tokenization can reduce barriers to entry and gradually improve secondary transfer capabilities. The next phase of tokenization will be determined by the quality of infrastructure: compliant distribution mechanisms, reliable settlement systems, and liquidity paths viable for both institutional investors and the on-chain ecosystem.

data.title: The Inflation Game of the US Dollar: The Fed's Policy Shift and the Crossroads of the Global Economy
data.description: Entering 2026, the global economy has not seen the expected calm, but rather has oscillated forward amidst multiple structural contradictions and policy uncertainties. At the beginning of the year, an unprecedented legal challenge against the Federal Reserve, like a boulder thrown into a calm lake, stirred up ripples regarding central bank independence, the direction of monetary policy, and even the political and economic landscape of the United States. This turmoil is by no means an isolated event, but rather a key entry point into the core dilemma of the current global economy—the stubbornness of inflation and the dilemma of monetary policy. This article will analyze the root causes of the difficulty in reinstating the "spirit" of US inflation based on recent economic dynamics and in-depth data, and explore how it intertwines with geopolitics, global capital flows, and the divergence among major economies, shaping the risk map for the coming year. I. The Shadow of Independence: The Political Storm Encountered by the Federal Reserve and Its Signaling Implications In early 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice's charges against the Federal Reserve and its Chairman Powell for alleged "fraudulent activities" in the construction of its new headquarters quickly transcended the scope of routine administrative disputes. Although the Federal Reserve swiftly responded that all operations were in compliance, the symbolic significance of this move was extremely dangerous. As many economists have pointed out, threatening a country's central bank leader with criminal charges is more common in regions with fragile systems, and this move undoubtedly casts a shadow over global investors' confidence in the stability of the U.S. system. The underlying motivation for this turmoil is generally considered to be closely related to monetary policy. The current administration continues to pressure the Federal Reserve to maintain an accommodative monetary environment, with its core demand being to keep interest rates low to support the massive fiscal deficit. In 2025, despite some easing of inflation, the US federal fiscal deficit remains high, and the issuance of Treasury bonds continues to climb, with short-term Treasury bills (T-bills) accounting for a significant proportion. When the Federal Reserve announced the end of quantitative tightening (QT) and planned to purchase hundreds of billions of dollars in Treasury bills, the market clearly saw the reappearance of "fiscal deficit monetization." The government's pressure on the central bank to directly or indirectly purchase Treasury bonds is essentially a process of debt monetization, directly increasing the base money supply and laying the groundwork for future inflationary pressures. This legal case can be seen as an extreme manifestation of political forces attempting to "discipline" the monetary authorities, forcing them to cooperate with the demands of fiscal expansion. II. The Return of Monetary Theory: Why Does the Inflation "Sprite" Refuse to Return to the Bottle? To understand the current inflation dilemma, we must return to the classic analytical framework of the quantity theory of money. In the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Federal Reserve's unprecedented monetary expansion led to the highest growth rate of the US money supply since 1913. According to the quantity theory of money (MV=PT), a rapid expansion of the money supply inevitably leads to a rise in the general price level. This theoretical prediction was precisely validated in 2022-2023, with the US CPI year-on-year growth rate surging to a forty-year high of 9.1%. Subsequently, the Federal Reserve initiated an aggressive cycle of interest rate hikes and quantitative tightening, causing a sharp drop in the growth rate of the money supply and a subsequent decline in the inflation rate. By the end of 2025, the CPI year-on-year growth rate had fallen to around 3%. However, a crucial turning point emerged at this point. Current data shows that the growth trajectory of the US money supply (measured by broad indicators such as M2) has leveled off, and even shows signs of bottoming out, rather than continuing to decline. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve's policy stance has undergone a subtle but crucial "dovish" shift: It announced the cessation of QT and a shift towards marginal easing: This signaled a shift in liquidity management to the market. Easing of Banking Regulation: Key regulatory requirements such as the Supplemental Leverage Ratio (SLR) are expected to be removed or adjusted in April 2026, which will unleash the lending capacity of the banking system and generate more money through the credit creation process. Rising Interest Rate Cut Expectations: The market has widely priced in the Federal Reserve starting an interest rate cut cycle in 2026. This series of operations signifies that the balance of monetary policy is shifting from prioritizing "fighting inflation" to "preventing recession" or "supporting fiscal policy." The monetary tap will be slightly turned back from its tight position. Historical experience shows that there is a lag effect of approximately 12-24 months between the rise and fall of inflation and changes in the growth rate of the money supply. The effects of monetary tightening in 2023-2024 have already manifested in 2024-2025, while the marginal easing that began at the end of 2025 is likely to gradually show its effects in the second half of 2026 and 2027. Therefore, core inflation (especially service inflation excluding volatile items) is far more sticky than expected, and after briefly approaching the 2% target, there is a real risk that the overall inflation rate will rise again. This is the monetary logic behind the so-called "inflation spirit is hard to put back in the bottle." III. Global Divergence: Inflationary Pressures in the US and Deflationary Challenges in China The global economy in 2026 presents a stark contrast, with fundamental differences in monetary policy being the dominant factor. The US: A Vortex of Inflation and Populist Politics Persistent inflationary pressures are directly eroding household purchasing power, and the "affordability crisis" has become the most pressing daily economic issue for ordinary American families. Although the job market has maintained some resilience, real wage growth has been partially offset by rising prices, leading to accumulating public discontent with the economy. Public opinion polls show that public approval ratings of the government's economic management capabilities remain low. This will directly impact the midterm elections in the fall of 2026. If inflation rebounds again before the election, public discontent may translate into punitive votes against the ruling party, bringing uncertainty to the political landscape. The US economic problem is essentially a distribution and stability dilemma under the pressure of "overheating." In contrast to the US, China faces major macroeconomic risks of insufficient demand and downward pressure on prices. Although the authorities have set a moderate target of "around 5% economic growth + around 2% inflation," achieving this is proving difficult. The core issue also lies on the monetary side: the growth rates of broad money supply (M2) and total social financing have consistently fallen below the "golden growth line" required for nominal GDP growth, reflecting weak credit demand and a declining velocity of money. This has led to sluggish Producer Price Index (PPI) and consumer goods prices, i.e., structural deflationary pressure. China's challenge lies in how to effectively boost domestic demand, unclog the monetary policy transmission mechanism, and address the long-term impacts of structural factors such as the real estate market adjustment without relying on large-scale stimulus. This divergence between US inflation and China's contraction has further misaligned the policy cycles of major central banks globally, exacerbating the complexity of international capital flows and volatility in exchange rate markets. IV. Crisis in the Margins: A Socio-Political Volcano Under Hyperinflation Besides major economies, the international focus at the beginning of 2026 also turned to two countries mired in hyperinflation—Iran and Venezuela—vividly demonstrating the catastrophic consequences of a collapsing monetary system. According to high-frequency tracking data, in January 2026, Venezuela's annualized inflation rate remained as high as 836%, continuing to lead the world; Iran's inflation rate also remained high at 84.6%. Such extreme price increases are essentially a monetary phenomenon of complete bankruptcy of the local currency's credit and uncontrolled money supply. It ruthlessly plunders the people's savings and purchasing power, becoming the root cause of social discontent. The recent ongoing protests in Iran due to currency devaluation and soaring living costs, and the turmoil triggered by the arrest of Venezuelan strongman Maduro, both stem from the underlying economic logic of hyperinflation. For these countries, any discussion of resource development (such as Venezuela's oil) or economic development must give way to a fundamental reconstruction of the monetary system. Historical experience has shown that in a hyperinflationary environment, the most direct and effective solution is a thorough reform of the monetary system, such as establishing a currency board system (like Hong Kong's linked exchange rate system) or complete dollarization (like Ecuador and El Salvador). By introducing an external hard currency anchor, inflationary expectations can be shattered in one fell swoop, restoring the basic function of economic calculation. Without the cornerstone of "stability," any investment and development is impossible. V. Market Paradox: Bubbles, Safe-Haven Assets, and the Myth of the US Dollar Amidst uncertainty in the real economy and policies, the global financial markets at the beginning of 2026 presented a seemingly contradictory picture: Following a strong rally in 2025, major US and global stock markets continued to climb in early 2026. Some technology stocks and metals sectors benefiting from the global energy transition (such as copper) performed particularly well. This creates a paradox: typically, the end of monetary tightening or expectations of an economic slowdown would depress the stock market, but the current market seems more focused on pricing in future easing expectations and structural growth stories such as artificial intelligence. It's worth noting that key valuation metrics are already at historical highs, and the market exhibits characteristics of "irrational exuberance." History shows that the bursting of stock market bubbles is often triggered by unexpected tightening of monetary policy. Current expectations of a shift towards easing may temporarily support the bubble, but its sustainability highly depends on whether corporate earnings can justify the high valuations and whether inflation can truly be "perfectly" controlled. The Shining of Safe-Haven Assets: Echoing the stock market's fervor, the prices of traditional safe-haven assets—gold and silver—both reached record highs in early 2026. This reflects, on the one hand, a deep-seated global concern about the credibility of fiat currencies (especially in addressing future inflation risks); on the other hand, escalating geopolitical risks and continued gold purchases by global central banks also provide solid support. The bull market in precious metals is a direct manifestation of hedging against "currency devaluation" and "uncertainty." The delicate balance between the US dollar and US Treasury bonds: Despite inflationary and fiscal pressures, the US dollar index is currently in a relatively strong range (e.g., around 1.17 against the euro). This is due to the US's growth and interest rate advantages relative to other major economies such as the Eurozone and Japan. However, with the start of the Fed's interest rate cut cycle and the increasingly prominent US "twin deficits" (fiscal and trade), the pressure for a medium- to long-term weakening of the US dollar is accumulating. The US Treasury market faces a "triple whammy": high supply, inflation eroding real returns, and the possibility that foreign investors may slow down their purchases due to political or exchange rate risks. Volatility in US Treasury yields may increase significantly. VI. 2026 Outlook: Key Clues Amid Uncertainty In summary, the direction of the global economy and markets in 2026 will revolve around several core clues: The Fed's "Tightrope Walking" Art: Can it strike a delicate balance between supporting the economy (or fiscal policy), managing financial market expectations, and ultimately controlling inflation? A misstep in any area could lead to sharp market volatility. The Interaction Between US Politics and Economics: How Will the Midterm Elections Affect Future Fiscal and Trade Policies? Will Rising Cost of Living Be a Decisive Factor in the Election? The Consequences of Policy Misalignment Among Major Economies: How Will the Different Policy Pace of the Four Major Central Banks—the US, Europe, China, and Japan—Drive Cross-Border Capital Flows and Trigger Readjustments in Exchange Rates and Asset Prices? The Overlap of Geopolitics and Energy Transition: From the turmoil in Venezuela and Iran to the reshaping of the global energy supply chain, geopolitical risks and the demand for green transition have jointly pushed up the central volatility of commodity prices. The Ultimate Test of Monetary Theory: After decades of neglect, the quantity theory of money is once again proving its explanatory power through global inflation and deflation. Understanding changes in the money supply remains the most crucial key to predicting economic cycles and asset price trends. As economists have advised, "It's all about money." In 2026, we may once again witness the power unleashed when the floodgates of currency are pushed and pulled by politics, fiscal policy, and market expectations. This power can inflate asset bubbles, ignite social unrest, and even determine the long-term trajectory of great power competition. For policymakers, investors, and ordinary citizens, recognizing the direction of monetary tides is one of the few certainties to prepare for in this uncertain era.

data.title: The Federal Reserve is expected to pause rate cuts, but the timing of further cuts remains unclear. The Waller vote is a key point of interest.
data.description:
Author: He Hao, Wall Street Insights

Nick Timiraos, a well-known financial journalist known as the "new Fed mouthpiece," wrote that Federal Reserve officials are expected to pause rate cuts this week for the first time since last September, choosing to hold rates steady after three consecutive rate cuts.

Timiraos stated that the Fed is expected to make only minor adjustments in its policy statement on Wednesday, maintaining the benchmark interest rate in the 3.5%–3.75% range. If Fed officials continue to use language about "further adjustments to interest rates," it will indicate that they are not yet ready to signal a long-term pause.

Timiraos points out that the more pressing question is: what circumstances would trigger the Fed to start cutting rates again?

data.title: CFTC Chairman's "Future Defense Plan" for Encrypted Oversight: Seeking Expanded Access Controls
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

On January 20, 2026, Mike Selig, the newly appointed chairman of the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), officially launched the "Future-Proof Initiative." This strategic move, aimed at restructuring the regulatory framework for digital assets, quickly attracted high attention from the global crypto industry, financial institutions, and regulators. As a core implementation action of the Trump administration's "pro-crypto" regulatory shift,this plan breaks the previous passive pattern of "enforcement instead of regulation" in the United States regarding crypto assets, attempting to balance innovation incentives and risk control by establishing stable and clear formal rules, while seeking the CFTC's core position in the field of crypto regulation.

data.title: Grayscale's BTC mini ETF launched; Morgan Stanley and Laser Digital apply for trust bank approval.
data.description:
Headline

▌Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF Now Available on Morgan Stanley Platform

Grayscale CEO Peter Mintzberg announced, "The Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (ticker symbol: BTC) is now available on the Morgan Stanley platform, opening investment access to over $7.4 trillion in assets under management (AUM)."

▌Nomura's Laser Digital Applies for US National Trust Bank License

Nomura Securities' digital asset division, Laser Digital, has applied for a national trust bank license with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), planning to be included in the federal banking regulatory system amid a more relaxed regulatory environment in the United States. This license will allow Laser Digital to operate nationwide without needing to apply for asset custody licenses on a state-by-state basis, but it does not involve accepting retail deposits and is expected to offer spot cryptocurrency trading services.

Blockchain Applications

▌Former Nasdaq Executive Giang Bui Joins Securitize as Vice President

Securitize has appointed former Nasdaq Head of US Equities and ETFs, Giang Bui, as its new Vice President of Issuer Growth. She will be responsible for driving "issuer-led compliant tokenization in the public and private markets." Bui previously worked at Nasdaq, the New York Stock Exchange, and the Chicago Board Options Exchange (Cboe), where she was involved in the listing of digital asset ETFs and market structuring. Securitize stated that the company plans to put its stocks on the blockchain in the first quarter of 2026, and Bui's addition will help issuers achieve clear, regulated ownership in the tokenized economy. Securitize manages approximately $4 billion in assets and has partnered with Wall Street institutions including Apollo, BlackRock, and BNY.

▌Tether Increases Gold Reserves by Approximately 27 Tons

According to Reuters, stablecoin issuer Tether disclosed that it increased its gold reserves by approximately 27 tons in the fourth quarter of 2025, roughly in line with analysts' estimates of 26 tons purchased in the third quarter. Tether stated that its gold reserves are now comparable to those of some sovereign central banks. For comparison, the Polish central bank increased its gold reserves by 35 tons during the same period, bringing its total reserves to 550 tons. Tether's USD stablecoin, USDT, currently has a circulating supply of $187 billion, while its gold token, XAUT, has a circulating value of $2.7 billion. As of the end of December last year, XAUT was backed by 16.2 tons of gold, accounting for 60% of the global supply of gold-backed stablecoins. The most recent audit, ending in September last year, showed that USDT's reserves included $12.9 billion worth of gold, equivalent to approximately 104 tons at the then-current gold price.

... According to Santiment data, the current market capitalization of stablecoins has shrunk by $2.24 billion in the past 10 days, with funds shifting towards gold and silver assets. Meanwhile, according to BitcoinTreasuries.NET, American Bitcoin Corp (stock code: $ABTC), a Bitcoin mining company backed by the Trump family, has added 416 Bitcoins, bringing its total Bitcoin holdings to 5,843. According to market news, Stelios Haji-Ioannou, the billionaire owner of EasyJet, has founded his own Bitcoin treasury company. Meanwhile, Jiuzi Holdings, a Nasdaq-listed Bitcoin treasury company, announced a strategic cooperation memorandum of understanding with Morgan International Finance Ltd., which plans to invest $90 million in Jiuzi through equity investment. The specific investment structure, lock-up period, and other arrangements will be determined after further negotiation and the signing of a legally binding final transaction document. The new funds will be used to support Jiuzi Holdings in advancing its blockchain and Web3 ecosystem expansion and implementing its digital asset strategy.

▌Coinbase Adds TRIA and ZAMA to Listing Roadmap

Coinbase has added Tria (TRIA) and Zama (ZAMA) to its listing roadmap.

▌Binance Wallet's Fifth Pre-TGE to List Zama (ZAMA)

Binance Wallet's fifth Pre-TGE Prime Sale will list Zama (ZAMA): Time: January 29, 2026, 16:00–18:00 (UTC+8) Eligibility: Must hold Binance Alpha points. Specific rules and event entry page will be announced soon.

... **Important Economic Developments** **Trump: Hopes Interest Rates Will Fall** Trump, when discussing his views on the Federal Reserve, expressed his hope that interest rates would fall. **Trump: Not Worried About Dollar Depreciation, Can Make It Fluctuate Like a Yo-Yo** US President Trump stated that he believes the dollar's depreciation is not significant, calling it "performing well," and anticipating fluctuations in its value. When asked by reporters if he was worried about the dollar's depreciation, Trump said, "No, I think the dollar is performing very well. I want it to return to its proper level, which is reasonable." Trump also hinted that he could manipulate the dollar's exchange rate, saying, "I can make it fluctuate like a yo-yo." However, he described this as an unfavorable outcome, comparing it to hiring unnecessary workers to manipulate employment data, while criticizing Japan's attempts to devalue its currency. The US dollar index (DXY) initially rose slightly after Trump's comments, but then fell sharply by more than 50 points, hitting a new low since February 2022. Non-US currencies rallied, with the offshore yuan briefly approaching the 6.93 level against the dollar, and gold continuing to rise to above $5180 per ounce. The US dollar index (DXY) fell by 1% intraday, breaking below the 96 level, reaching a new low since February 2022. In terms of news, when asked if he wanted to see the dollar depreciate further, Trump said the dollar might swing up and down like a "yo-yo."

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged this week is 97.2%

According to CME's "FedWatch": The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in January is 2.8%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 97.2%. By March, the probability of a cumulative rate cut of 25 basis points is 17.4%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 82.2%, and the probability of a cumulative rate cut of 50 basis points is 0.4%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌How AI in Cryptocurrency Trading Reshapes and Disrupts the Role of Humans

Artificial intelligence (AI) is gradually being integrated into cryptocurrency trading, accelerating the analysis, execution, and optimization processes previously performed manually. Investors and trading firms are facing the challenge of automating decision-making without compromising control, accountability, or human judgment.

... While some projects are striving to build more autonomous trading systems, most AI tools in the cryptocurrency space remain heavily constrained. Humans still bear responsibility for strategy development, risk setting, and outcome accountability, while machines handle the majority of bandwidth for data-intensive tasks such as research and monitoring. In the cryptocurrency market, the balance between automation and regulation is quietly reshaping trading workflows and beginning to redefine which human roles remain important. AI hasn't eliminated humans from the cryptocurrency space, but it's reshaping the distribution of labor across the entire industry. This shift is largely happening quietly, at the task level, particularly in research roles that previously relied on teams of junior analysts and interns. As trade execution becomes increasingly automated, traders can focus their energy on strategy and risk control rather than manual operations.

data.title: Tokenization unlocks private credit
data.description:
Author: Zeus   Translator: Block unicorn

Private lending is borrowing money without going through banks. Individuals and businesses don't need to go to commercial banks; instead, they borrow from private lending institutions. These institutions can be investment funds, specialized lending institutions, or financial companies. Borrowers get the funds they need, and lending institutions earn interest for taking on the risk. It's that simple. That's the whole idea behind private lending.

This market exists because banks don't lend to everyone. Banks have strict regulations, lengthy processes, and many loan applications are rejected. Private lending institutions fill the gap left by banks, but because the risk is higher, the funds are usually locked up for a longer period, so they charge higher interest rates.

This is why the yield on private lending is usually higher than that of savings accounts or government bonds. Someone is paying for this yield; it doesn't come out of thin air.

For a long time, private lending has been difficult to access.

You need substantial capital to enter, and your funds are often locked up for years. Selling your position is difficult, and once invested, you have limited access to information. This benefits large investors but excludes most. Tokenization doesn't change the nature of private lending; it changes how it operates. Loans still exist in the real world, legal contracts still exist, and borrowers still repay loans. The difference is that ownership is no longer tracked through paper documents and difficult-to-access systems, but digitally on the blockchain. You can think of it this way: the loan itself hasn't changed; only the system surrounding it has been upgraded. This is why private lending has become one of the largest real-world assets (RWAs) on-chain. More money flows on-chain through lending than through gold, stocks, or real estate. This is because investors focus on stable income and scalability. For a simple example… Imagine someone taking out a loan using a home as collateral, and these loans are bundled together. Investors invest in this portfolio and earn interest as the homeowner repays the loan. Tokenization allows investors to hold their shares digitally, providing clearer visibility into repayments and sometimes making buying and selling easier. Another example is private credit funds. These funds lend to numerous borrowers simultaneously. Tokenization allows for smaller investment sizes and easier access, while still requiring funds to adhere to standard rules and regulations. Tokenization can streamline the onboarding process, reduce administrative work, speed things up, and increase transparency. However, it doesn't eliminate risk. If a borrower stops making payments, there will be losses. If many people withdraw simultaneously, liquidity can disappear. Tokenized private credit is still essentially lending.
